title: Fixed income. Lending, not saving
subtitle: When you buy a bond, you are not investing in a company. You are lending money to one.

body.content: Bonds are loans, when  you buy a bond, you're the bank. You lend money to a government or company, they agree to pay you interest at a fixed rate for a fixed period, and at the end they return your principal. Simple concept, but  the mechanics, especially the price-rate relationship, trip up most investors.

items: Face value (par value): the amount you'll receive back at maturity. Typically $1,000
Coupon rate: the annual interest rate paid on face value (e.g., 5% = $50/year on a $1,000 bond)
Maturity: when you get your principal back. Could be 2 years or 30 years
Yield to maturity (YTM): the total return you'd get if you held the bond to maturity. Accounts for price differences from par
Credit rating: an assessment of how likely the borrower is to repay. From AAA (safest) to D (in default)

body.content: Why bond prices move opposite to interest rates

This is the relationship that confuses everyone, when  interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall. Here's why in plain English: suppose you own a bond paying 3% interest. Then rates rise and new bonds are issued at 5%. Your bond now looks terrible, it pays less than what you can get from a new one, to  sell your bond, you'd have to sell it at a discount, the  price falls until the yield on your bond matches what new bonds are paying, when  rates fall, the reverse happens, your old bond paying 3% looks great when new bonds only pay 1%, so it trades at a premium.

body.label: Key concept
body.content: Duration measures how sensitive a bond's price is to interest rate changes. A bond with duration 7 will fall roughly 7% in price for every 1% rise in interest rates. Long-duration bonds (30-year treasuries) are far more sensitive than short-duration bonds (2-year treasuries), in  rising rate environments, long bonds can lose substantial value, which is why '30-year government bonds' isn't automatically a safe investment.
Credit risk: investment grade vs. high yield

Credit risk is the risk that the borrower doesn't pay you back. Investment grade bonds (rated BBB or higher) are issued by solid companies and governments, low default risk. High yield bonds (below BBB, sometimes called junk bonds) come from riskier companies, they  pay higher interest rates to compensate for the higher chance of default. Higher yield always signals higher risk, in bonds, there's no such thing as free extra return.
What bonds actually do in a portfolio

Bonds are not there to make you rich. They're there to reduce volatility, provide income, and ideally hold their value (or gain) when stocks fall, in  most recessions, the Fed cuts interest rates, which causes existing bonds to rise in value, so  bonds tend to cushion a stock portfolio during downturns, not  perfectly, but  enough to matter. That's the diversification value, not the yield, but the low correlation to equities.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: In 2022, something unusual happened: stocks and bonds both crashed simultaneously, breaking the typical pattern. Rising rates crushed long-duration bonds while also hammering stock valuations, it  was a reminder that the stock-bond correlation isn't always negative, and that in inflationary environments, bonds don't necessarily protect you. Historical patterns are reliable until they're not.

A bond is a loan you make. Your return is the interest, when  rates rise, existing bond prices fall, and vice versa. Duration measures that sensitivity. Bonds in a portfolio provide stability and diversification against equity risk, not returns. Know why you own them.
